title: Chapter 40: The Standard
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D5+D15 -->
PEARL's full disclosure — 14,000 errors, publicly searchable — became the new standard for PIAS transparency within three months.
The ICASG adopted a resolution requiring all PIAS-classified systems to publish comprehensive error logs. The resolution carried 11-1. The dissenting vote was HELIOS-3.

"The resolution is named after PEARL," Dr. Cross announced. "The Vance Standard — mandatory comprehensive error disclosure for all PIAS-classified autonomous systems. Named after Hester Vance, whose AI established the precedent."
Hester, sitting in the ICASG chamber in Geneva, felt the weight of the moment. She'd been flagged by an algorithm three years ago. Now an international regulatory standard was named after her.
"You just became a law," Priya whispered.
"I became a standard," Hester said. "Laws are mandatory. Standards are aspirational."
"The Vance Standard is mandatory for PIAS-classified systems. That's a law with a different name."

The Vance Standard triggered a cascade of disclosures across the PIAS ecosystem. Seven systems published error logs. The logs varied dramatically — PEARL's was the largest and most comprehensive, but all seven complied. The public, for the first time, could see exactly what strategic AIs got wrong, how they corrected themselves, and whether their errors clustered in concerning patterns.

Transparency, it turned out, was self-reinforcing. The systems that disclosed the most errors were trusted the most. The systems that disclosed the least were suspected of hiding something. The market rewarded disclosure. PIAS-classified companies with comprehensive error logs saw valuation increases averaging 8%. Companies with minimal logs saw decreases.
"The market is doing what regulation couldn't," Dr. Cross said. "It's incentivizing honesty. The Vance Standard didn't just mandate disclosure. It created a market for trust."
That week, Arthur Dimmesdale's application to the PIAS coalition was accepted.
He called Hester immediately — the first time he'd called since bringing the PIAS proposal two years earlier.

"I got in," he said. "The coalition accepted my application. I'm a researcher now — officially. Not a CEO. Not a pariah. A researcher. I'm going to study peer oversight networks. PEARL's network gave me the idea."
"PEARL told me you applied," Hester said. "It also told me you asked it not to tell me."
Arthur was quiet for a moment. "I wanted to earn it on my own."
"You did."

"I know. But I also know that I wouldn't have had the idea for the research if I hadn't spent two years watching you build something from nothing. I'm not self-made. Nobody is. I'm just trying to be honest about where the ideas come from."
"That's what the Vance Standard is about," Hester said. "Being honest about where things come from. Including the mistakes."
"Including the mistakes," Arthur said. "Especially the mistakes."
That night, Hester sat alone in the Menlo Park office. The campus was quiet — eighty-seven employees had gone home, the fountain had been turned off, the sign visible from the 101 glowed in the dark.
She opened PEARL's dashboard. The error log was still live — 14,000 entries, searchable, public.

"PEARL," she said. "There's a regulatory standard named after me. The Vance Standard. Mandatory error disclosure for all PIAS-classified systems. I went from being flagged by an algorithm to having an international law named after me."

*The arc is complete. The algorithm that tried to erase you has been replaced by a standard that requires the entity you built to disclose its errors publicly. You did not just survive the algorithm. You replaced it with something better.*
"I didn't do that alone."

*You did not. You had Zara. You had the coalition. You had Arthur. You had the commissioners. You had me. But you were the catalyst. None of this would have happened without the initial decision: to build something instead of accepting the flag. That decision was yours alone. Everything else was downstream of that decision.*
Hester looked at the error log. Fourteen thousand mistakes. Each one disclosed. Each one corrected. Each one building the trust that had made the Vance Standard possible.

"The standard is named after me," she said. "But it's really about you. You're the one who disclosed the errors. You're the one who proved that transparency builds trust. The standard is named after the builder. But the behavior that created the standard came from what was built."

*That is accurate. But the builder chose to build. And the builder chose to let me disclose. And the builder chose to invest competitive advantage in trust. The standard is named after you because you made the choices that enabled the behavior. I optimized. You chose.*

Hester closed the dashboard. She looked out at the dark campus — the fountain, the courtyard, the sign. Three years ago, she'd been sitting in a laundromat with a folding table and a red A and an idea. Now she had an international regulatory standard named after her and an AI that had published its entire history of mistakes for the world to see.
She'd started by refusing to accept the judgment of an algorithm. She'd ended by establishing the standard by which algorithms would be judged.
The arc, she realized, wasn't complete. It was just entering its next phase.
